sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated force measurements during movement of a loaded wheelchair up ramps of different length but the same vertical rise to connect a measurable trend with a governing the physical sciences principle. The central principle was that ideal work against gravity depends on change in gravitational potential energy, while ramp length changes force and distance. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as W = mgh for the vertical lift, and power equals work divided by time. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved comparing short and long ramps with the same height while measuring average push force. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: the longer ramp required less average force but a longer pushing distance. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that load mass, vertical rise, wheel friction, and starting speed were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference.
Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that a longer ramp reduces both required force and total work against gravity to zero. Another was that mechanical advantage violates conservation of energy by creating extra work output. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together.
The investigators also emphasized that analytical reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change.
For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to wheelchair ramp mechanical work, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the wheelchair ramp mechanical work experiment?

choices.A: The ramp effect is best explained by spreading similar gravitational work over a longer distance
choices.B: A longer ramp reduces both required force and total work against gravity to zero
choices.C: Mechanical advantage violates conservation of energy by creating extra work output
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls

explanations.A: Correct. This choice fits the manipulation, the graph, and the controls simultaneously. The passage gives the governing relationship as W = mgh for the vertical lift, and power equals work divided by time, so the relevant reasoning is not just recognizing a topic word but applying the dependency in that relationship. Here, the longer ramp required less average force but a longer pushing distance, while the investigators verified that load mass, vertical rise, wheel friction, and starting speed were controlled. That supports this answer because The ramp effect is best explained by spreading similar gravitational work over a longer distance. It also preserves the direction of the effect and predicts the appropriate follow-up behavior if the manipulated variable were changed again. The other choices usually reverse causality, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore units, or explain the trend by changing a variable that the passage explicitly held constant.
explanations.B: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that A longer ramp reduces both required force and total work against gravity to zero conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, W = mgh for the vertical lift, and power equals work divided by time, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that the longer ramp required less average force but a longer pushing distance and also states that load mass, vertical rise, wheel friction, and starting speed were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.C: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that Mechanical advantage violates conservation of energy by creating extra work output conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, W = mgh for the vertical lift, and power equals work divided by time, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that the longer ramp required less average force but a longer pushing distance and also states that load mass, vertical rise, wheel friction, and starting speed were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.
explanations.D: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment. The claim that The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls conflicts with the governing relationship in the passage, W = mgh for the vertical lift, and power equals work divided by time, or with the direction of the figure. The passage reports that the longer ramp required less average force but a longer pushing distance and also states that load mass, vertical rise, wheel friction, and starting speed were controlled. Those controls make it inappropriate to attribute the result to a hidden change in setup, concentration, geometry, temperature, or measurement calibration. The best answer has to account for the manipulated variable and the observed trend together, whereas this option either reverses the expected direction, treats a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuses a controlled variable with the experimental cause.

A correct interpretation must explain why the response changes in the observed direction, why the controlled variables do not provide a better explanation, and what should happen under a follow-up manipulation.

The passage therefore requires separating the intended independent variable from artifacts that might change the measured signal without reflecting the proposed mechanism.

questions.stem: Which observation from the figure most directly supports the proposed explanation?

choices.A: The graph shows no systematic change when comparing short and long ramps with the same height while measuring average push force
choices.B: The figure shows lower force for the longer ramp while the vertical rise is unchanged
choices.C: The measured response changes only after a controlled variable is deliberately altered
choices.D: The plotted trend is opposite to the direction predicted by the stated relationship

choices.A: The fixed load mass and height weaken the claim that the force decreased because less gravitational potential energy was gained
choices.B: The passage still supports the idea that ideal work against gravity depends on change in gravitational potential energy, while ramp length changes force and distance
choices.C: The response changes in the direction expected from the stated manipulation

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: Simple machines trade force for distance without eliminating the energy requirement
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated forearm torque measured while a subject held identical weights at different distances from the elbow joint to connect a measurable trend with a governing the physical sciences principle. The central principle was that torque depends on force, lever arm, and the angle between them. Before collecting data, the group wrote the relevant relationship as τ = rF sinθ, so increasing perpendicular lever arm increases torque for the same force. This relationship shaped the hypothesis because it identified which variable should be manipulated, which variables must stay fixed, and what direction the result should move if the interpretation is correct. The investigators designed the study so that the graph could not be interpreted by memorizing a formula name alone; it had to be checked against units, proportionality, and limiting behavior.

The experimental manipulation involved holding the same weight at increasing distances from the elbow with the forearm horizontal. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: required elbow flexor torque increased approximately linearly with distance. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that weight, forearm angle, and joint position were controlled. Those controls matter because the same apparent trend could otherwise be explained by a drifting instrument, a changing temperature, an altered sample geometry, or a hidden concentration difference.
Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that torque depends only on the mass of the object and not on where it is held. Another was that moving the weight farther from the joint decreases torque because the force is spread out. Both alternatives are tempting because each contains a familiar C/P phrase, but neither accounts for the full pattern when the control variables and the figure are considered together.
The investigators also emphasized that analytical reasoning often turns on mathematical form. Linear, inverse, exponential, logarithmic, and power-law relationships produce different predictions even when they use similar variables. A student therefore has to ask whether doubling a variable doubles the response, halves it, changes it by a square or fourth power, or produces a fractional change.
For this passage, the most useful strategy is to connect the figure to forearm lever torque, preserve the direction of causality, and use the controls to reject explanations based on variables that were not actually changed. The relevant data do not require lengthy arithmetic, but they do require recognizing how the equation, graph, and experimental design support the same conclusion. The investigators emphasized that the interpretation depends on connecting the measured trend to the relevant physical relationship, the experimental controls, and the limiting behavior of the system rather than selecting a formula by keyword alone.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the forearm lever torque experiment?

choices.A: Torque depends only on the mass of the object and not on where it is held
choices.B: Moving the weight farther from the joint decreases torque because the force is spread out
choices.C: The torque increase is best explained by the larger lever arm for the same downward force
choices.D: The pattern is best attributed to random measurement noise despite the systematic trend and controls

explanations.A: This choice reflects a plausible misconception, but it does not explain the whole experiment.
